questions.text: I feel energized when people follow my lead.
I proactively identify risks at the start of any new undertaking.
I often wait for the group to signal readiness before pursuing change.
I pick up on the implicit social contracts operating in different cultural contexts.
I hold the view that personal growth should be a daily practice.
I say yes to compelling opportunities even if they don't fit my current priorities.
I craft each communication fresh because templates feel impersonal to me.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering has a moral authority that overrides most other considerations.
I experience the surprise of being startled not as a brief jolt but as a lingering rush of adrenaline.
I notice that emotional events pass through me rather than sticking — like water off a smooth surface.
I find that maintaining composure in the presence of strong emotions from others enables me to provide the kind of help that is actually needed.
I enjoy negotiating as an equal partner.
I feel that reserving my best energy for my own creative work is non-negotiable.
I hold the view that any award or recognition I receive belongs partly to everyone who supported me.
I would move to a less desirable place if it let me be more useful to people who need help.
I find that I am often unaware of the social significance of gestures and mannerisms.
I see my daily routine as a series of intentional choices, not defaults.
I believe that treating others' suffering as morally compelling — even when I am tired or distracted — is a discipline that reflects my real values.
I tend to miss when someone is trying to end a conversation politely.
I feel responsible for keeping a group connected and cohesive.
I find that I thrive when I give myself permission to focus on what I want.
I notice when someone deliberately echoes the vocabulary of the most powerful person in the room.
When I'm in the grip of a strong emotion, the world outside of it seems to temporarily cease to exist.
I tend to pick up the tab without mentioning it.
I am able to assess the severity of someone's situation without my assessment being colored by emotional involvement.
I feel that hesitation costs more than imperfect action.
I keep my workspace meticulously organized because it helps me think clearly.
I swap seasonal items when the weather actually changes rather than on a set date.
I notice when someone speeds up their speech to get through a point before they can be interrupted.
I notice that my system takes much longer than others' to recalibrate after a disruptive event.
I hold the view that my low reactivity gives me a kind of durability in the face of life's difficulties.
I find that I can hold the weight of bad news without it visibly affecting me.
I rarely pick up on the nonverbal negotiation that happens before a group makes a decision.
I am the person who usually calls the meeting or sets the agenda.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the measure by which I judge whether my life is being lived with integrity.
I find that I can work through emotionally heavy material — accounts of trauma, loss, injustice — without it altering my focus.
